title: Data Interview Fundamentals
description: A compact practice set that mirrors the style of common analytics and data science screening rounds.
instructions: Use this set for short interview-style practice across analytics, experimentation, and ML basics.
Some questions have more than one correct answer.
Answer review includes explanations after you submit.

sections.title: Analytics, SQL, and Metrics
sections.description: Foundational questions that show up in practical data interviews.

questions.prompt: Which SQL clause is used to filter aggregated results after GROUP BY?
questions.explanation: WHERE filters rows before aggregation, while HAVING filters aggregated groups after GROUP BY.

questions.prompt: Which checks are useful before trusting an A/B test result?

options.label: Verify sample size is large enough
Ignore variance if the average improved
Review whether the metric matches the business goal

questions.explanation: Good A/B test review includes power, randomization, and metric relevance. Ignoring variance is unsafe.

questions.prompt: What metric describes the percentage of users who completed a desired action out of all users who were exposed to the experience?
questions.explanation: Conversion rate is the share of exposed users who completed the target action.

sections.title: Modeling and Reasoning
sections.description: Short concept checks for data science and applied ML interviews.

questions.prompt: If a classifier performs well on training data but poorly on unseen validation data, what is the most likely issue?
questions.explanation: A large performance gap between train and validation data is a classic sign of overfitting.

questions.prompt: A column contains hundreds of possible city names with a long tail of rare values. Which encoding is usually the safer first choice for a tree-based model?

options.label: One-hot encode every city value without review
Group rare values and encode carefully
Convert the city string directly to an integer rank

questions.explanation: Grouping rare levels helps reduce noise and cardinality while keeping meaningful signal.

questions.prompt: Increasing model complexity can reduce bias but may increase variance.
questions.explanation: This is the standard bias-variance tradeoff. More flexible models can fit better but may generalize worse.
